questions.questionText: A patient presents with sudden onset severe eye pain, headache, nausea, and sees halos around lights. Eye examination shows a mid-dilated fixed pupil and shallow anterior chamber. IOP is 50 mmHg. Diagnosis?
questions.optionB: Acute angle-closure glaucoma (acute attack — pupillary block → rise in IOP → corneal edema → halos; ophthalmological emergency)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Acute ACG = painful red eye + halos + mid-dilated fixed pupil + high IOP = emergency. Why correct: Pupillary block → aqueous humor trapped behind iris → iris bows forward → angle closure → acute IOP elevation. Features: severe eye pain, headache, nausea/vomiting (mimics acute abdomen), halos (corneal edema), mid-dilated non-reactive pupil, shallow AC, conjunctival injection, corneal edema. Why A wrong: POAG — chronic, painless, insidious, visual field loss detected late ('silent thief of sight'). Why C wrong: Secondary — due to other eye disease (uveitis, lens changes, steroid use). Why D wrong: Uveitis — painful but pupil is miotic (not dilated), keratic precipitates, cells in AC. Clinical relevance: Treatment: IV mannitol + oral acetazolamide + topical timolol + topical pilocarpine 2% + anti-inflammatory. Definitive: laser peripheral iridotomy (both eyes). Exam trap: POAG = chronic, painless; ACG = acute, painful. POAG is MORE COMMON but ACG is the emergency. Memory aid: 'Acute ACG = Agonizing pain, Mid-dilated pupil, Emergency IOP lowering needed.' Reference: Khurana – Glaucoma.
questions.conceptTag: Acute ACG angle-closure emergency mid-dilated pupil IOP
